text: I present to you a robotic transgastric GIST resection. This is a ninety-one-year-old male who presented to the emergency room with GI bleeding. He required four units of PAC-RED blood cell transfusion. EGD showed a submucosal mass near the gastricardia. This is a picture of the lesion. I apologize for not having the original EGD image. This picture was taken with a robotic scope. As you can see, the lesion was fairly close to the G-junction located next to the gastricardia within the body of the stomach.

The surgeon enters the abdomen to the left of the umbilicus using a 5mm port with a zero-degree laparoscope, followed by placing an 8mm port in the left lower quadrant for gastric fixation. A third 5mm port is placed in the right lower quadrant for a long Dorsey clamp to occlude the duodenum, minimizing gastric inflation loss. The clamp is secured to the table using a common table clamp.

The surgeon places a single 2-0 Vicryl suture through the mid gastric body using a Carter-Thomason device to secure the stomach to the abdominal wall, providing counter traction for port placement. The stomach is inflated, and the surgeon uses a local anesthetic needle to verify the planned trajectory of the ports. The first transgastric port is placed in the left upper quadrant.

The surgeon struggles to fully insert the first port into the stomach due to its elasticity, despite full insufflation. The port insertion is guided by EGD, and the surgeon attempts to push the port further in. After successfully inserting the first port, the surgeon places the second port, causing the first port to dislodge as the stomach moves away from the abdominal wall. The surgeon refines the gastrotomy and reinserts the first port. The third port is inserted without much difficulty, with all three ports spaced approximately five centimeters apart.

The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand and a vessel sealer in the right hand to perform the resection of a pedunculated lesion near the GE junction. The lesion appears to arise from the muscularis in the superior portion of its attachment to the stomach. The resection is performed smoothly, with a full thickness resection near the superior attachment and division of the mucosa around the inferior portion of the lesion. The operative site remains stable throughout the procedure.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic insufflator attached to a robotic port to maintain gastric insufflation. A Dorsey clamp is applied externally on the duodenum to prevent loss of insufflation. The surgeon performs a full thickness resection of the lesion, creating a gastrotomy in the posterior wall of the stomach. Initially, there is no loss of gastric insufflation, but CO₂ eventually escapes into the abdominal cavity, causing a loss of working space. The surgeon opens a laparoscopic port and attaches it to a smoke evacuator to restore gastric insufflation.

The robotic vessel sealer approaches the gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) from various angles, facilitating its complete resection in a confined space. The posterior gastrotomy is visible, and the gastric rugae become more prominent as the gastric distension decreases.

The surgeon opens the abdominal ports to desufflate the abdomen, allowing gastric insufflation through the robotic ports to restore stomach distension. Using a 90-day V-Lock suture, the surgeon performs a full-thickness single-layer closure of the gastric defect, ensuring not to encroach upon the GE junction, which is identified with the EGD.

The surgeon completes the closure of the gastric defect using a V-lock suture, securing it with two additional throws. A small blood vessel is inadvertently pierced, causing minor bleeding. The lesion, measuring 5x5x3 centimeters, is placed in an eight-millimeter laparoscopic endocatch bag and removed transorally with a biopsy forcep through the EGD.

The surgeon checks the closure for gaps, observing a small amount of blood at the GE junction. Gastric distension is lost after removing the EGD, prompting reinsertion of the scope to re-insufflate the stomach. The robotic suction irrigator is used to clear gastric fluid, revealing a small mucosal defect inferiorly. A 3-0 Vicryl figure-of-eight suture is placed to oppose the mucosal edges.

A small tear is visible in the anterior portion of the GE junction with an adhering clot, but no active bleeding is observed. Three ports are withdrawn from the stomach, and the holes are closed using figure-of-eight sutures. The stomach appears intact post-closure.
